text:
This paper tries to assess the evolution and present day approaches to judicial decision-making in international and hybrid criminal courts. It sets out to do so in a broader context where international law has moved at a rapid pace from one of almost complete impunity to a highly institutionalised system of justice. And this pace of judicialization, in whatever way one chooses to understand the term, has not been accomplished without a certain number of tensions and controversies on the effectiveness and legitimacy of international justice.

The author examines how these judges – who had no practical jurisprudence to work from – grappled with a plethora of legal and factual issues when they engaged in what the author labels “constructionist” adjudication, in particular in relation to new heads of liability that tested the old maxim nullum crimen sine lege. The author then explores the requirements of independence and impartiality of the international judiciary, focusing on how the inner workings of institutions and the pressure from outside actors including whistle-blowing scandals and the deferral of a trial at the ICTY and SCSL have seemed to undermine the perception of their impartiality. The article then engages with the applicable law for the ad judicanda determination as well as with the social consequences of high-stakes adjudication that has the effect of undermining the reliability and impartiality of the exercise of international justice.
explanation: The passage reads like a polished, abstract-style summary with cohesive, broad statements and some phrasing often seen in AI-generated text. It includes a few generic academic clichés and a couple of unusual Latin/legal terms that can appear in AI-produced content.
